images.caption: Figure 1:Comparison between (a) a uniform research automation pipeline that applies identical processing to all users and yields homogeneous outputs, and (b) NanoResearch, which recognizes distinct researcher personas and provides personalized skills and feedback upon failure, enabling each persona to evolve along its own trajectory.

Figure 2:The NanoResearch framework.An Orchestrator𝒪\mathcal{O}processes a personalized research request and coordinates a three-stage pipeline (ideation, experimentation, writing) to produce a publication-ready paper. A Skill Bank𝒮\mathcal{S}, a Memory Moduleℳ\mathcal{M}, and policy learning jointly accumulate experience and drive self-evolution across cycles.
Figure 3:Composition of our benchmark.The 20 research tasks span seven domains, and cover a wide variety of subtasks (left), with dataset sizes ranging from∼\sim5K to over 1M samples (right).

Figure 5:Case study on UCI HAR: three simulated users withConservative,Practical, andExploratorytastes
lead visibly different blueprints, code, and paper claims at every stage.

Figure 6:All pages of the system-generated sensor time-series paperMSGDCN: Reproducible Lightweight Gated Dilated Convolutions
for Sensor Time-Series Classification.

Figure 7:All pages of the system-generated tabular regression paperRELM-DReCal: Residual-Enhanced Linear Regression with Learned
Feature Recalibration.

Figure 8:All pages of the system-generated keyword spotting paperCSC-Net: Reproducible Channel-Split Convolutions for
Lightweight Keyword Spotting.
Figure 9:Architecture diagram for Profile A (Evidence-First Scientist).
Figure 10:Architecture diagram for Profile B (Ablation-Focused Researcher).
Figure 11:Architecture diagram for Profile C (Benchmark-Driven Exploratory Researcher).
